<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: spinoza-verification-and-proof-carrying-claims
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/spinoza-verification-and-proof-carrying-claims.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/spinoza-verification-and-proof-carrying-claims.qmd@verification-review-merge
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Proof-Carrying Claims and Adversarial Review

Claim ledgers remember what the system believes. Proof-carrying claims ask what
has to happen before one of those beliefs is allowed to become stronger.
Adversarial review handles the cases where one verifier is not enough.

The point is not to turn every sentence into a theorem. Most important AI
claims need a more varied discipline. Some need a formal proof. Some need a
citation dossier. Some need a replay log, executable procedure, benchmark
record, or human sign-off. Some need a tribunal because the evidence is
contested, the interpretation is ambiguous, or the stakes are too high for one
review path.

The reader throughline is simple: stronger claims should leave stronger
evidence trails, and failed review should be remembered.

That sentence matters because proof and review language can become rhetorical.
A theorem tag, citation bundle, replay log, or committee verdict can make a
weak claim look finished. The ASI Stack uses the opposite rule. Every strong
label should narrow the claim, record what was checked, preserve what failed,
and state what the result is allowed to change.

This is the last epistemic gate before the book returns to execution. Context
adequacy says whether a claim had enough checking capacity. Claim ledgers say
what the system believes and how that belief changed. Proof-carrying claims
and adversarial review decide which selected claims have earned stronger
standing and which must remain blocked.

## Problem

Generated prose can sound justified before it is justified.

A model can propose a claim, a proof sketch, a citation, a code path, a replay
story, a benchmark interpretation, or a review verdict. None of those proposals
is verification by itself. The system still has to ask what the claim means,
what tier of support it is seeking, what artifact would count, who or what
checked the artifact, what failed, who dissented, and what the result is
allowed to change.

Claim ledgers give the claim an identity. Verification envelopes give the claim
an inspection path. A claim enters with a requested support tier, an
interpretation mapping, a candidate justification artifact, and a consumer
context. It leaves with a scoped update, no update, downgrade, block, required
action, residual, or escalation. Passing a verifier is not the only meaningful
outcome. Failure, timeout, mismatch, and dissent are evidence too.

This is why proof-carrying claims and adversarial review belong together. A
ledger without verification can become bookkeeping around unsupported belief.
Verification without a ledger can produce isolated artifacts that no later
reader knows how to use. Review without a state change can become commentary.
The merged layer connects all three.

The useful result is not a badge that says "verified." It is a scoped ledger
effect: no change, downgrade, revision, block, residual, required action, or
narrow acceptance.

## Why Existing Approaches Are Insufficient

Neural generation is not a verifier, and self-critique is not a tribunal.

A model can produce a formal-looking object that proves the wrong target. It
can cite a source that is adjacent but not supportive. It can write a convincing
procedure without recording the execution environment. It can criticize its own
answer while preserving the same blind spots that produced the answer. Multiple
reviewers can also collapse into consensus theater when they share the same
context, incentives, prompt structure, or hidden evidence gap.

External formal-methods and contestability work give the right comparison
points. Proof-Carrying Code pairs executable artifacts with machine-checkable
evidence against a host policy. Lean gives the proof-assistant vocabulary for
propositions, records, inductive types, tactics, and proof terms. Contestable
AI design literature points to the need for challenge, appeal, audit, and
review surfaces. Autoformalization work shows why translating prose into a
formal target is itself a risky step. Debate frames adversarial review as a
way for competing systems to surface what a judge might miss. LLM-as-judge
work shows that model graders can be useful while still carrying position,
verbosity, self-enhancement, reasoning, and calibration limits. Those
references ground the vocabulary, but they do not make this repository a
Proof-Carrying Code implementation, a complete theorem prover, an
autoformalizer, a debate system, an LLM-judge benchmark, or a validated
institution.

The hard boundary is interpretation. A proof can be valid and still not mean
what the prose claim meant. A source can be real and still not support the
reading attached to it. A replay log can be faithful and still depend on
environment assumptions. A tribunal can agree and still be wrong. The record
therefore has to keep interpretation mapping, artifact validity, semantic
adequacy, reviewer dissent, and support-state effect separate.

## Core Claim

Selected claims and artifacts should move through proof-carrying,
justification-carrying, or adversarial-review envelopes that record tier,
interpretation mapping, evidence dossier, verifier or tribunal result, dissent,
limitations, failed attempts, required actions, residuals, and ledger effects.
This remains an architectural argument.

The source basis is bounded. Spinoza supplies the core discipline: neural
systems may propose, but structured verifiers decide; formal scope is explicit;
outward claims carry tiers; failed, timed-out, or unknown results do not become
success. GenesisCode supplies the obligation-envelope pattern for executable
artifacts. UAT supplies the retrieval-bounded review pattern: dossiers, atomic
propositions, adversarial siege, cycle caps, and human checkpoints. Talos
supplies the execution pressure: review outputs have to become typed,
auditable artifacts with claim graphs, evidence sets, proof bundles, logs,
replay, residual risk, and human adjudication. Verification Bandwidth explains
why tribunal escalation can be the right answer to inadequate verification
workspace. TreeLLM and Coherence Exchange remain useful context, but neither
turns the present repository into a deployed verifier.

The live chapter core claim stays at `argument` support. The repository has
schemas, synthetic fixtures, local harnesses, and finite Lean predicates over
record fields. It does not have an open-domain verifier, proof generator,
citation validator, semantic-equivalence checker, reviewer-independence audit,
LLM-judge calibration result, debate run, validated verdict-correctness result,
runtime trace, or whole-system epistemic correctness result.

## Mechanism

The mechanism begins when a claim or artifact asks for stronger standing.

The first move is route selection. The system decides whether the claim needs a
formal proof, citation dossier, executable procedure, replay artifact,
benchmark record, tribunal review, human sign-off, downgrade route, or block.
The choice depends on the claim's risk, scope, requested support state, and
consumer.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Claim ledger or artifact"] --> B["Verification route"]
  B --> C["Proof, citation, procedure, replay, benchmark, or tribunal"]
  C --> D["Result, failure, mismatch, dissent"]
  D --> E["Bounded ledger effect"]
  E --> F["No change, downgrade, revise, block, scoped accept, or residual"]
```

The second move is interpretation. A formal proof, source citation, replay log,
or tribunal dossier only matters after the system records what it is supposed
to mean. The record should name the prose claim, formal target or evidence
object, confidence in the mapping, known limitations, failed attempts, and
non-claims.

The third move is adversarial pressure when ordinary verification is not
enough. A tribunal-style record defines the dossier, assigns roles, runs
probes, preserves dissent, and issues scoped actions. It can accept a claim
within a boundary, require revision, block dispatch, narrow authority, request
source expansion, demand human sign-off, or create residual work.

The final move is ledger writeback. The result changes the claim ledger or
execution constraints only within the recorded scope. A verifier pass may
create a scoped update rather than a broad promotion. A failed artifact may
become a durable failed-attempt record. A mismatch may route to tribunal
review. A missing artifact may block the requested tier. The system records
what happened so the same overclaim cannot return under cleaner prose.

## A Claim-Review Scenario

Suppose the stack produces this claim: "the new replacement controller
preserves rollback readiness." That sentence is too broad to accept because it
sounds like both a design statement and an execution result. A verification
envelope starts by splitting it.

One smaller claim may be formal: a finite transaction record contains a rollback
owner, prior artifact reference, trigger condition, dry-run status, and monitor
window. That target can go to a schema, validator, or Lean predicate. Another
claim may be procedural: a dry run actually happened in a named environment.
That needs a replay log or procedure record. A third claim may be interpretive:
the dry run is adequate for the deployment being discussed. That may need
adversarial review or human sign-off.

If the finite predicate passes but the replay log is missing, the ledger records
a narrow pass and a procedural blocker. If the replay log exists but the
environment does not match the proposed deployment, the result becomes semantic
mismatch or residual. If reviewers disagree about adequacy, the dissent stays
attached and the claim can be narrowed rather than promoted. The sentence may
be rewritten as "the synthetic transaction record includes rollback metadata,"
while the broader rollback-readiness claim remains unpromoted.

That is the chapter in miniature. The system is allowed to use proofs,
citations, logs, judges, and tribunals, but each one changes only the claim it
actually checked. This scenario is not a real verifier-output tier assignment,
not a citation-truth result, not a semantic-equivalence result, not a tribunal
quality result, and not a support-state promotion.

## Interfaces

The claim ledger supplies stable identity, support state, provenance,
contradiction links, revision history, and promotion blockers. Verification
events do not replace that substrate. They write bounded effects into it.

A proof-carrying claim record needs fields such as `claim_id`, `claim_scope`,
`required_tier`, `justification_type`, `interpretation_mapping`,
`justification_artifact`, `verifier_result`, `failed_attempt_refs`,
`formal_scope`, `limitations`, `claim_validity_effect`, `residual_route`,
`ledger_update`, `support_state_effect`, and `non_claims`.

A tribunal review record needs fields such as `review_id`, `target_ref`,
`risk_class`, `dossier_boundary`, `dossier_refs`, `reviewer_roles`,
`adversarial_probes`, `prior_review_refs`, `unchanged_evidence_guard`,
`findings`, `evidence_refs`, `dissent`, `unresolved_issues`, `verdict`,
`required_actions`, `constraint_effects`, `human_adjudication`, and
`non_claims`.

Those records connect to VCM for evidence objects, verification bandwidth for
adequacy and risk, Lean or GenesisCode-style tools for formal or executable
checks, UAT-style review for contested cases, and Labor OS for downstream work
constraints.

## Invariants

The requested tier is explicit.

Formal tiers require formal artifacts. Citation tiers require citation or
dossier artifacts. Procedure tiers require procedure logs or schema fixtures.

Verifier failures, timeouts, and mismatches are non-promotional.

Dissent remains visible.

High-risk artifacts cannot bypass required tribunal review.

Prior review over unchanged evidence cannot quietly reverse a rejection into
acceptance.

Those invariants stop the most common laundering path. A model cannot say that
a proof was attempted and therefore the claim is stronger. It has to show the
artifact, verifier result, interpretation mapping, formal scope, limitations,
dissent, support-state effect, and non-claims.

The finite Lean hooks support this record-level boundary. They prove that
modeled records obey small gates: formal support tiers require appropriate
artifact references, failed verifier results block or downgrade rather than
promote, tribunal verdicts include reviewer roles and dissent, and high-risk
artifacts cannot be accepted when required tribunal review is absent. They do
not prove that natural-language formalization is correct, that arbitrary
theorems are valid, that citations are accurate, that reviewers are
independent, or that verdicts are correct.

## Failure Modes

Certified delusion is the headline failure. The system has a formal-looking
object, but the object certifies the wrong claim.

Invalid formalization is the interpretation failure. The theorem or procedure
may be coherent, but the mapping from prose to artifact is wrong, too narrow,
or missing an important condition.

Justification absence is the tier failure. A claim asks for a support tier
without the artifact that tier requires.

Theorem laundering uses a narrow theorem to promote a broad systems claim.

Consensus theater records agreement without evidence-linked findings.

Critique without action produces review prose but no ledger effect, required
action, blocker, downgrade, or residual.

Repeated-review laundering sends unchanged evidence through review until the
language sounds balanced enough to pass.

The verification layer exists to make all of those failures visible. If it
cannot, proof and review are not protecting the book or the system.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable implementation is a record surface for verification events,
not an open-domain verifier or working tribunal.

The repository already has part of that slice. The proof-carrying-claim
fixtures validate record shape for claim scope, justification type,
interpretation confidence, verifier artifact refs, failed-attempt refs, formal
scope, source refs, support-state effect, and non-claims. The tribunal-review
fixtures validate bounded dossiers, reviewer roles, adversarial probes,
findings, evidence refs, dissent, unresolved issues, verdicts, required
actions, and human adjudication. The local harnesses include expected-invalid
cases for overpromotion, missing artifacts, prior-review laundering, missing
probes, and hidden dissent.

That is useful, but narrow. These are synthetic record-discipline harnesses.
They do not validate the referenced procedure, discharge a proof, check
citation truth, prove semantic equivalence, measure reviewer independence, or
run a deployed tribunal.

The adversarial review dossier and verdict-quality probe adds a bounded
synthetic version of that next slice. It checks two synthetic review dossiers:
one scoped acceptance that keeps dissent and unresolved deployment limits
attached, and one semantic-mismatch rejection that blocks promotion until the
claim boundary is rewritten. It also rejects seven bad paths: missing dossier
refs, LLM-judge-only acceptance, missing adversarial probes, dissent whose
scope constraint is erased, action verdicts without required actions,
prior-review reuse without a guard, and support-state promotion from the
fixture. This is not a deployed tribunal result.

## Beyond the State of the Art

At product maturity, this layer becomes a verification operating system for
high-value claims.

It would not try to formalize every sentence. It would decide which claims
deserve stronger treatment, choose the right justification type, record the
interpretation mapping, run the relevant verifier or review process, and write
the result back to the claim ledger without overpromoting the prose claim.

Routine claims would stay cheap. Load-bearing claims would pay for proof,
citations, replay, procedure checks, benchmark review, or human sign-off.
High-risk or contested claims would pay for adversarial review. The result
would be a stack that spends rigor where rigor changes permissions, release
readiness, dispatch, authority, or public claims.

The endpoint is not "AI proves everything." The endpoint is anti-laundering
epistemic machinery: stronger claims require stronger artifacts, failures stay
visible, dissent remains attached, and every support-state effect travels
through a record that can be audited.

## Summary

Proof-carrying claims and adversarial review are one verification surface. The
claim ledger says what the claim is and how its belief state changes. This
layer says what kind of proof, citation, procedure, replay, benchmark,
tribunal, or human-review record is adequate before a selected claim or
artifact can ask for stronger standing.

Some claims need proof. Some need citations. Some need procedures, replay
logs, benchmarks, or tribunal review. Every route needs a record that names
its scope, artifact, failed attempts, limitations, dissent, and allowed ledger
effect. Without that record, stronger-sounding justification can become a
laundering channel.

Durable claim identity and belief revision remain the job of the claim ledger.
Runtime action remains downstream in Labor OS and typed jobs.

The boundary is intentionally conservative. A proof, citation dossier, replay
log, benchmark packet, tribunal verdict, or human sign-off changes only the
claim it actually checked.

## Handoff

Verification only matters when it changes what the system can do. Once a
verification event narrows a claim, blocks a route, requires source fetch,
demands human sign-off, or creates residual work, the execution system needs
typed jobs that preserve those constraints.

That is the job of **Labor OS and Typed Jobs**.
